question: A patient develops acute hypotension with signs of massive intra-abdominal bleeding and is taken emergently to the operating room. At exploratory laparotomy there is approximately 2 L of hemoperitoneum and ongoing hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation. Based on the imaging provided, which operative approach is most appropriate?

options.A: Emergency open laparotomy with aortic control and splenectomy
options.C: Proximal splenic artery ligation with spleen preservation
options.D: Laparoscopic splenic artery repair and preservation

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image for relevant findings. This is an axial contrast-enhanced CT of the upper abdomen. At the splenic hilum there is a focal, contrast-filled saccular outpouching (arrow) arising where the splenic artery courses to the spleen, consistent with a splenic artery aneurysm. There is higher-attenuation fluid tracking around the spleen and in the peritoneal spaces, compatible with hemoperitoneum.

Next, I relate those imaging findings to the clinical scenario. The vignette states the patient has approximately 2 L of hemoperitoneum and ongoing hemodynamic instability despite resuscitation, i.e., active, life-threatening intra-abdominal hemorrhage from a likely ruptured splenic artery aneurysm identified on CT.

Then, I consider how the operative choices fit that combination of imaging and physiology. In an unstable patient with massive intra-abdominal bleeding from a visibly ruptured splenic artery aneurysm, rapid and definitive hemorrhage control is required. Endovascular coil embolization (Option B) typically requires time, angiographic access, and relative hemodynamic stability and therefore is not optimal for ongoing exsanguination. Laparoscopic repair or spleen-preserving ligation (Options C and D) are impractical in the setting of massive hemorrhage and poor visualization. Nonoperative observation (Option E) is contraindicated given the volume of blood loss and instability.

Finally, open laparotomy with rapid proximal vascular control (including aortic control if needed) and splenectomy (Option A) provides the fastest and most definitive means to control hemorrhage from a ruptured splenic artery aneurysm identified at the splenic hilum and to evacuate the hemoperitoneum. This approach best matches the imaging and the patient’s unstable physiology.
caption: Abdominal CT scan with IV contrast showing a thin neck connecting the splenic artery and the aneurysm.
sub_caption: Abdominal CT scan with IV contrast showing a thin neck connecting the splenic artery and the aneurysm.
image_context: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a 5-cm splenic artery aneurysm at the splenic hilum (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-21-e919956-g001">Figure 1</xref>) with intramural thrombosis and surrounding soft-tissue density, with minimal free fluid (impending rupture) () with intramural thrombosis and surrounding soft-tissue density, with minimal free fluid (impending rupture) (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-21-e919956-g002">Figure 2</xref>). The left kidney was noted to be ectopic and rotated in the left lower lumbar region. Shortly afterward, the patient developed tachycardia with hypotension. The Rapid Response Team (RRT) was activated and the patient was resuscitated with IV fluids and stabilized, but soon thereafter he collapsed with hypovolemic shock. A surgical consultation was sought and, following further resuscitation, the patient was immediately taken to the operating room (OR) with the diagnosis of a ruptured splenic artery aneurysm. He underwent emergency exploratory laparotomy, with the finding of a large hemoperitoneum of about 2 L. Upon entering the peritoneal cavity, the patient became significantly hypotensive (60/40 mmHg) and was deteriorating fast. Abdominal aortic cross-clamping was achieved at the level below the diaphragm until the patient was partially stabilized hemodynamically. The neck of the ruptured splenic artery aneurysm was found and an en block excision of the tail of the pancreas, spleen, and the aneurysm were performed. Peritoneal cavity drains were left in place.). The left kidney was noted to be ectopic and rotated in the left lower lumbar region. Abdominal aortic cross-clamping was achieved at the level below the diaphragm until the patient was partially stabilized hemodynamically. The neck of the ruptured splenic artery aneurysm was found and an en block excision of the tail of the pancreas, spleen, and the aneurysm were performed. Peritoneal cavity drains were left in place.
